lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I have sent to seek him, and to find the body.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: How dangerous is it that this man goes loose!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Yet must not we put the strong law on him:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: He’s loved of the distracted multitude,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Who like not in their judgment, but their eyes:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And where ’tis so, the offender’s scourge is weigh’d,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But never the offence. To bear all smooth and even,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: This sudden sending him away must seem

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Deliberate pause: diseases desperate grown

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: By desperate appliance are relieved,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Or not at all.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Where the dead body is bestow’d, my lord,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: We cannot get from him.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But where is he?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Without, my lord; guarded, to know your pleasure.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Bring him before us.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Ho, Guildenstern! bring in my lord.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Now, Hamlet, where’s Polonius?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: At supper.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: At supper! where?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Not where he eats, but where he is eaten: a certain convocation

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of politic worms are e’en at him. Your worm is your only

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: emperor for diet: we fat all creatures else to fat us, and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: we fat ourselves for maggots: your fat king and your lean beggar

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: is but variable service, two dishes, but to one table: that’s the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: end.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Alas, alas!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A man may fish with the worm that hath eat of a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: king, and eat of the fish that hath fed of that worm.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: What dost you mean by this?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nothing but to show you how a king may go a progress

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: through the guts of a beggar.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Where is Polonius?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In heaven; send hither to see: if your messenger find him not

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: there, seek him i’ the other place yourself. But indeed, if you

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: find him not within this month, you shall nose him as you

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: go up the stairs into the lobby.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Go seek him there. To some Attendants.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He will stay till ye come.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Hamlet, this deed, for thine especial safety—

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Which we do tender, as we dearly grieve

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For that which thou hast done—must send thee hence

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: With fiery quickness: therefore prepare thyself;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The bark is ready, and the wind at help,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The associates tend, and every thing is bent

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For England.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For England!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Ay, Hamlet.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Good.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: So is it, if thou knew’st our purposes.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I see a cherub that sees them. But, come; for England! Farewell, dear mother.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thy loving father, Hamlet.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My mother: father and mother is man and wife; man and wife

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: is one flesh; and so, my mother. Come, for England!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Follow him at foot; tempt him with speed aboard;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Delay it not; I’ll have him hence to-night:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Away! for every thing is seal’d and done

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That else leans on the affair: pray you, make haste.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And, England, if my love thou hold’st at aught—

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: As my great power thereof may give thee sense,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Since yet thy cicatrice looks raw and red

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: After the Danish sword, and thy free awe

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Pays homage to us—thou mayst not coldly set

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Our sovereign process; which imports at full,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: By letters congruing to that effect,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The present death of Hamlet. Do it, England;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For like the hectic in my blood he rages,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And thou must cure me: till I know ’tis done,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Howe’er my haps, my joys were ne’er begun.

glosses.phrase: Who like not in their judgment, but their eyes
glosses.definition: who judge by appearance, not thought
the worm that eats corpses (memento mori)

glosses.phrase: politic worms are e’en at him
glosses.definition: worms that govern like politicians are at him now

glosses.phrase: two dishes, but to one table
glosses.definition: two courses at one feast (for worms)
the King of England (addressed as a vassal)

annotations.title: 'Your worm is your only emperor for diet'
annotations.body: Hamlet's lecture to Claudius on worms, kings, and beggars is one of the play's great meditations on mortality: 'we fat all creatures else to fat us, and we fat ourselves for maggots.' The argument moves through the food chain and arrives at a leveling truth: a king may travel, via a fish, through the guts of a beggar. The rhetoric is grotesque but philosophically precise — it dismantles the doctrine of the king's two bodies. A king is meat like any other meat. The speech is Hamlet's cruelest to Claudius, because it is delivered in the code of madness the King must accept while understanding perfectly its content. Only in Act 5, in the graveyard, will Hamlet return to this meditation with a different spirit.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: The sealed commission and the secret plot
annotations.body: Claudius's final soliloquy reveals what the audience has suspected and the play has not yet confirmed: the letter Hamlet carries to England is a death warrant. The monarch who in 3.3 could not pray effectively now directs the pen with cold efficiency. The lines 'thou must cure me: till I know 'tis done, / Howe'er my haps, my joys were ne'er begun' are a small masterpiece of compromised hope. Shakespeare holds two portraits of Claudius in superposition throughout the play — the penitent and the manager — and the second is what we see here. The commission will become the hinge of Act 5, when Hamlet, aboard ship, discovers and rewrites it.
sources: Geoffrey Bullough, Narrative and Dramatic Sources of Shakespeare, vol. 7 (1973)

trials.prompt: What is Hamlet's parable of the 'politic worms,' the fisherman, and the fish meant to demonstrate?
options: That worms will inherit the earth.
That death levels kings and beggars: a monarch may end in a beggar's belly.
That fishing is a noble art.
That Denmark is being eaten away by corrupt politicians.

trials.prompt: Where does Hamlet finally say Polonius may be found?
options: In the castle cellars, preserved in salt.
In heaven — and if the messenger does not find him there, he should check hell.
Buried beneath the orchard where old Hamlet died.
In the chapel where Claudius prays.

trials.prompt: What does Claudius's soliloquy at the end of the scene reveal?
options: That he has repented of his crime and will confess.
That the sealed commission to England instructs the English king to execute Hamlet at once.
That Gertrude has joined the plot against Hamlet.
That Polonius is not dead but hiding.

trials.prompt: What is the function of the comparison Claudius makes between Denmark's claim on England and a 'hectic' fever?
options: It suggests that English politics are feverishly disordered.
It frames Hamlet's execution as a kind of political medicine — a treatment for a raging illness within Claudius himself.
It is a joke at the expense of the English king's cowardice.
It indicates that Hamlet has contracted a fever in the voyage.
